concept_name: Specific Heats Cp and Cv of Gases
locked_facts: Cv = (f/2)R where f = degrees of freedom (R = 8.314 J/mol·K)
Cp > Cv because at constant P, gas expands and does work on surroundings
At constant V: all heat goes to internal energy. At constant P: heat goes to internal energy + work
truth_statements: Cp is always greater than Cv because heating at constant pressure requires extra energy for the gas to do expansion work
The difference Cp - Cv = R is universal for ideal gases regardless of the number of atoms
The ratio γ = Cp/Cv determines the speed of sound in a gas and the steepness of adiabatic processes
Monatomic gases have the highest γ (5/3) because they have the fewest degrees of freedom
The specific heats of gases can be predicted from the equipartition theorem using the degrees of freedom

aha_moment.what_to_show: Heating a gas at constant V vs constant P. At constant V: piston locked, ALL heat raises temperature. At constant P: piston free to move, gas expands doing work — some heat goes to work, rest to temperature. So Cp > Cv by exactly R (the work done per mole per degree).
aha_moment.visual_emphasis: Two containers: locked piston (all heat → ΔU) vs free piston (heat → ΔU + work). Energy allocation bars compared.
aha_moment.why_this_breaks_misconception: Students don't understand WHY Cp > Cv — seeing the energy split into internal energy vs work makes it obvious

simulation_states.label: Heating at constant V
simulation_states.description: Gas in sealed container. Heat Q added. No expansion (ΔV = 0, W = 0). All heat goes to internal energy: Q = nCvΔT. Temperature rises efficiently.
simulation_states.emphasis: At constant V: no work done, all heat raises temperature

simulation_states.label: Heating at constant P
simulation_states.description: Gas in container with free piston. Heat Q added. Gas expands, doing work W = PΔV = nRΔT. Extra heat needed: Q = nCpΔT = nCvΔT + nRΔT.
simulation_states.emphasis: At constant P: some heat does work → need MORE heat for same ΔT → Cp > Cv

simulation_states.label: Degrees of freedom
simulation_states.description: Monatomic (3 DOF): Cv = 3R/2, γ = 5/3. Diatomic (5 DOF): Cv = 5R/2, γ = 7/5. More DOF = more ways to store energy = lower γ.
simulation_states.emphasis: More degrees of freedom = higher Cv = lower γ. Monatomic gases have the highest γ.

simulation_states.label: Interactive
simulation_states.description: Student selects gas type (mono/di/poly). Cp, Cv, γ calculated. Heat gas at constant V vs constant P — compare temperature rise for same heat input.
simulation_states.emphasis: Same heat input: constant V gives bigger ΔT than constant P. The difference is the work.

common_misconceptions.belief: Cp and Cv differ because the gas absorbs different amounts of heat
common_misconceptions.correction: The gas CAN absorb any amount of heat in both cases. Cp > Cv because at constant pressure the gas must do extra work (PΔV = nRΔT) as it expands. This extra energy comes from the additional heat input, not from a different absorption mechanism.
common_misconceptions.simulation_emphasis: Show energy flow diagram: at constant P, heat splits into ΔU + work; at constant V, all heat goes to ΔU

common_misconceptions.belief: γ is the same for all gases
common_misconceptions.correction: γ = 1 + 2/f depends on the degrees of freedom: monatomic (γ = 5/3 ≈ 1.67), diatomic (γ = 7/5 = 1.4), polyatomic (γ ≈ 1.3). Different molecular structures have different γ.
common_misconceptions.simulation_emphasis: Bar chart comparing γ for He, N₂, CO₂ — visibly different values

legend.meaning: Specific heat at constant V = fR/2
Specific heat at constant P = Cv + R
